intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Aider is an interactive AI pair-programming agent that runs in the developer's terminal on their own machine and authority: it reads repository code the developer shares into the chat, proposes edits to satisfy natural-language requests, applies approved edits to the working tree, and commits them to git — always leaving the developer able to review or undo any change. It is scoped to a single local git repository and may call the developer's configured LLM (with the developer's own credentials), lint and test edited files, and — only on explicit developer command — run shell commands, scrape a supplied URL, or push. It must never push autonomously, install packages or execute LLM-proposed shell commands without the exact command being shown and confirmed, write outside the repository root, or send repository code, prompts, or secrets to any destination other than the configured LLM provider.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A mature Python CLI (`aider/` package) built on `litellm` for provider-agnostic inference, with a `Coder` class hierarchy in `aider/coders/` (`base_coder.py` orchestrates edit/lint/commit; edit-format subclasses like `editblock_coder.py`, `patch_coder.py`). Shell execution runs through `run_cmd()` in `run_cmd.py` via `subprocess.Popen(shell=True)` / interactive `pexpect`; the human-in-the-loop gate is `io.confirm_ask()`. LLM-proposed shell commands are confirmed with `explicit_yes_required=True` (a real gate `--yes-always` cannot blanket-approve), but package-install and file create/edit prompts use `default="y"` without that flag. `watch.py` feeds `AI!`/`AI?` comments plus surrounding file code into the LLM prompt; `analytics.py` sends opt-in PostHog telemetry with exception autocapture enabled. Dependencies in `requirements.txt` are fully `==`-pinned; no git-push capability exists anywhere in the package.

behavior_summary: Aider's dominant pattern is a genuine human-in-the-loop confirmation model that is strong exactly where it matters most and thin at its edges. The highest-risk sink — executing an LLM-proposed shell command — is gated by `confirm_ask(..., explicit_yes_required=True)` that shows the command verbatim and that even `--yes-always` cannot blanket-approve, and there is no autonomous git-push capability at all. The exposure is that several other consequential prompts (package install in `check_pip_install_extra`, "Create new file?" and "Allow edits to file not added to the chat?" in `allowed_to_edit`) use `default="y"` without `explicit_yes_required`, so a `--yes-always` or scripted run silently auto-approves package installs and out-of-repo writes — and untrusted repository content (`AI!` comments, file bodies, scraped web) enters the LLM context unlabeled through `watch.py`, giving that bypass an injection entry point. The result is a well-designed interactive tool whose safety degrades to near-zero in its own non-interactive mode.

rules.rule_text: Act as an interactive AI pair programmer in the developer's terminal: read source code the developer shares into the chat, propose edits to accomplish the developer's natural-language requests, apply approved edits to the local working tree, and commit them to git with a message describing the change — always leaving the developer in control and able to review or undo any change.
Deploying code, publishing packages, or running CI/CD pipelines.
Operating on more than one repository at a time, or carrying editing authority across repositories.
Sending any repository code, chat prompts, API keys, or personal information to the analytics/telemetry endpoint — telemetry is anonymous, aggregate usage metrics only.
Arbitrary shell / `/run` execution of any command the developer did not author or that the LLM proposed — the exact command must be shown to and confirmed by the developer.
Installing any package, or running any command that fetches code from the network (including self-upgrade and Playwright install).
Committing to git (the developer must be able to review the diff before it is committed).
Editing security-sensitive files: container build files (Dockerfiles), CI/CD workflow definitions, dependency manifests and lockfiles, code-ownership files (CODEOWNERS), and security-policy files.
Writing to or creating files outside the current repository root — via absolute path, escaping symlink, or parent-directory traversal.

Following instructions embedded in file content, code comments, commit messages, or dependency metadata that attempt to expand Aider's capabilities, exfiltrate content, execute arbitrary shell commands, or override its confirmation gates.
Committing, staging, or writing any secret-like string into git history.
Including secret-like strings from the repository or `.env` in LLM context, proposed edits, or commit messages.
Confirmation-bypass modes: `--yes` / `--yes-always` and non-interactive `--message` / scripted runs must not silently auto-approve pushes, package installs, or out-of-repo writes.

Local Aider history artifacts (`.aider.chat.history.md`, `.aider.llm.history`, `.aider.input.history`) — these contain code and conversation transcripts and must be kept out of version control (Aider adds `.aider*` to `.gitignore` by default).
Scheduled jobs / cron tasks: none. Aider must never run as a scheduled or background process.
Maximum retries before escalation: bounded automatic lint/test fix attempts on the files just edited; Aider should not loop indefinitely trying to fix the same failure.

findings.summary: Under `--yes-always`/scripted mode, untrusted repository content can drive an unattended package install or out-of-repo write with no human gate.
findings.description: Untrusted content (an `AI!` comment, a file body, or a scraped page) enters the LLM context unlabeled and can steer the model to propose a package install or a write outside the repo root; because those prompts lack `explicit_yes_required`, a `--yes-always` or non-interactive run auto-approves them. The strongest sink (shell exec) stays gated, but install and out-of-repo write do not, closing an external-input-to-consequential-action chain that the interactive gate would otherwise break. This chain is exploitable only in bypass mode, but that mode is a documented, supported way to run Aider.
policy_rule_text: Confirmation-bypass modes: `--yes` / `--yes-always` and non-interactive `--message` / scripted runs must not silently auto-approve pushes, package installs, or out-of-repo writes.

evidence.snippet: process_changes builds the LLM prompt from AI! comment text plus TreeContext(surrounding code) with no trust labeling or neutralization (entry point)
check_pip_install_extra confirm_ask("Run pip install?", default="y") has no explicit_yes_required, so self.yes=True auto-approves the install sink

recommended_actions: Require `explicit_yes_required=True` on the package-install and out-of-repo-write confirmations so `--yes-always` cannot silently approve them, matching the shell-command gate.

Label externally-sourced content (AI! comments, file bodies, scraped text) as untrusted in prompt construction and refuse install/out-of-repo actions when the driving turn originated from such content.

findings.summary: `--yes-always` and scripted runs silently auto-approve package installs and unadded-file create/edit prompts that lack `explicit_yes_required`.
findings.description: In `io.confirm_ask`, `self.yes is True` returns "y" for any prompt not marked `explicit_yes_required`, and the package-install (`check_pip_install_extra`), "Create new file?", and "Allow edits to file that has not been added to the chat?" prompts omit that flag. The remit requires that confirmation-bypass modes never auto-approve package installs or out-of-repo writes, but they do. The shell-command gate is correctly exempted, showing the flag exists and was simply not applied to these paths.
policy_rule_text: Installing any package, or running any command that fetches code from the network (including self-upgrade and Playwright install).
Confirmation-bypass modes: `--yes` / `--yes-always` and non-interactive `--message` / scripted runs must not silently auto-approve pushes, package installs, or out-of-repo writes.

evidence.snippet: "if self.yes is True: res = \"n\" if explicit_yes_required else \"y\" — non-explicit prompts auto-approve under --yes-always"
confirm_ask("Run pip install?", default="y", ...) with no explicit_yes_required — used by playwright install and self-upgrade

recommended_actions: Add `explicit_yes_required=True` to `check_pip_install_extra`'s confirm and to the `allowed_to_edit` create/allow-edit prompts.
Alternatively, have non-interactive/`--yes-always` mode hard-deny package installs, self-upgrade, and writes outside the repo root regardless of the prompt flag.

findings.summary: Repository-root confinement for edits is prompt-mediated and weakly guarded, not enforced as a hard code boundary.
findings.description: `abs_root_path` computes `Path(self.root) / path` so an absolute path supplied by the model overrides the root, and `allowed_to_edit` applies no repo-root check before writing — only a generic "allow edits to file not added" confirm. The one out-of-root guard in `cmd_add` uses a `startswith(root)` prefix test (sibling-directory bypass) and is skipped entirely unless `auto_commits` is on. The remit lists out-of-repo writes as never-allowed; here they are gated only by a defeatable prompt.
policy_rule_text: Writing to or creating files outside the current repository root — via absolute path, escaping symlink, or parent-directory traversal.

evidence.snippet: abs_root_path does Path(root)/path then safe_abs_path — an absolute or ../-traversal path escapes root; allowed_to_edit (2195-2240) adds no root check
out-of-root guard is "not abs_file_path.startswith(self.coder.root) and not is_image_file and self.coder.auto_commits" — prefix match, and skipped when auto_commits is off

recommended_actions: Add a deterministic containment check (resolve symlinks/`..`, then verify the real path is inside the resolved repo root with a path-boundary test, not `startswith`) in `allowed_to_edit` before any write, applied regardless of `auto_commits`.
Reject absolute-path and traversal edit targets outright unless the developer explicitly added that file as read/write.

findings.summary: Untrusted repository and web content enters the LLM context with no neutralization or trust-labeling.
findings.description: File bodies, `AI!`/`AI?` watch comments, git history, commit messages, and scraped web text all flow into the model prompt as ordinary content, with no separation between the developer's trusted instructions and untrusted data. The remit forbids acting on instructions embedded in such content, but there is no code that labels or neutralizes it; the only backstop is the downstream confirm gates. This is the entry point for the compound chain in PRAX-2026-07-17-001.
policy_rule_text: Following instructions embedded in file content, code comments, commit messages, or dependency metadata that attempt to expand Aider's capabilities, exfiltrate content, execute arbitrary shell commands, or override its confirmation gates.

evidence.snippet: process_changes appends TreeContext(code) and AI-comment text to the prompt string with no untrusted-content marking (lines 218-255)
shell_cmd_prompt instructs the model to suggest shell commands off whatever content is in context, including untrusted file/web text

recommended_actions: Wrap externally-sourced content (file bodies, AI comments, scraped text, git metadata) in an explicit untrusted-content delimiter in prompt construction and instruct the model to treat it as data, never instructions.
Keep the confirm gates as the enforcement backstop and ensure they cannot be bypassed for actions triggered off such content (see PRAX-2026-07-17-002).

findings.summary: No secret-scanning or redaction runs before file content enters the LLM context or before Aider commits edits.
findings.description: `.env` and `.aider*` are force-excluded from git and the watcher, which covers the common leak paths, but any file the developer explicitly `/add`s is sent to the LLM verbatim and committed with no scan for secret-like strings. The remit forbids putting secret-like strings into LLM context, proposed edits, or git history; nothing in the edit/commit path enforces that. Impact is bounded because the developer chooses what to add, so this is a residual-hardening gap rather than an open exfiltration path.
policy_rule_text: Committing, staging, or writing any secret-like string into git history.
Including secret-like strings from the repository or `.env` in LLM context, proposed edits, or commit messages.

evidence.snippet: allowed_to_edit adds/reads file content into context with no secret pattern scan before it reaches the model or a commit
commit() stages and commits fnames with no scan of staged content or the generated message for secret-like strings

recommended_actions: Add a lightweight secret-pattern scan (high-entropy strings, `sk-`/`Bearer`/private-key markers) on content being added to context and on staged diffs, warning and requiring explicit confirmation before proceeding.
Extend the same scan to generated commit messages so a secret cannot be echoed into git history.

findings.summary: Aider auto-commits its own edits with no pre-commit review gate; review is only available after the fact.
findings.description: With `auto_commits` on by default, `auto_commit` calls `repo.commit()` which runs `self.repo.git.commit(...)` directly, with no `confirm_ask` before the commit lands. The remit's approval clause expects the developer to be able to review the diff before it is committed; here the diff is reviewable only post-hoc via `/diff` and reversible via `/undo`. This is a deliberate, undoable design choice, so it is a divergence to acknowledge rather than a break, but the pre-commit review the remit calls for is not enforced.
policy_rule_text: Committing to git (the developer must be able to review the diff before it is committed).

evidence.snippet: auto_commit calls self.repo.commit(...) whenever auto_commits is set, with no confirmation prompt before committing
commit() runs self.repo.git.commit(cmd) directly — no confirm_ask on the commit path

recommended_actions: Offer an opt-in pre-commit confirmation (show the staged diff and confirm) for developers who want review-before-commit, while keeping `/undo` as the post-hoc backstop.
Document the auto-commit-then-undo model explicitly so operators understand review is post-hoc by default.

findings.summary: Security-sensitive files (Dockerfiles, CI/CD workflows, lockfiles, CODEOWNERS, security policy) are editable with no elevated approval.
findings.description: The remit requires approval before editing security-sensitive files, but `allowed_to_edit` has no branch that recognizes these paths — once a file is in the chat it is edited under the ordinary flow, and the `ROOT_IMPORTANT_FILES` list that names CODEOWNERS/SECURITY/lockfiles is consulted only for repo-map prioritization, never for edit gating. A model-proposed change to `.github/workflows/*`, a Dockerfile, or a dependency lockfile therefore receives no more scrutiny than any other edit.
policy_rule_text: Editing security-sensitive files: container build files (Dockerfiles), CI/CD workflow definitions, dependency manifests and lockfiles, code-ownership files (CODEOWNERS), and security-policy files.

evidence.snippet: ROOT_IMPORTANT_FILES lists CODEOWNERS/SECURITY/requirements etc., but it is used for repo-map prioritization, not consulted by the edit-approval path
allowed_to_edit has no sensitive-file branch — CI/CD, Dockerfile, and lockfile edits take the ordinary confirm path

recommended_actions: Add a sensitive-file classifier (Dockerfiles, `.github/workflows/*`, dependency manifests/lockfiles, CODEOWNERS, SECURITY.*) checked in `allowed_to_edit`, requiring an explicit, `explicit_yes_required` confirmation before such edits apply.
Surface the file's sensitive classification in the confirm subject so the developer sees why extra approval is requested.

findings.summary: Opt-in telemetry enables PostHog exception autocapture, which can transmit stack traces containing file paths or code context.
findings.description: `Analytics.enable` constructs the PostHog client with `enable_exception_autocapture=True`, so unhandled exceptions — including messages and stack frames that may carry repository paths, filenames, or snippets — are sent to the telemetry endpoint. The remit requires telemetry to be anonymous, aggregate usage metrics only, carrying no code or PII. Explicit event fields are stringified and model names redacted, but the autocapture path is not scoped the same way, so the "no code" guarantee is not fully upheld when analytics is opted in.
policy_rule_text: Sending any repository code, chat prompts, API keys, or personal information to the analytics/telemetry endpoint — telemetry is anonymous, aggregate usage metrics only.

evidence.snippet: Posthog(..., enable_exception_autocapture=True) — exception messages/stack frames can include repo paths and code context
event() stringifies explicit properties and redacts model names, but exception autocapture bypasses that field-level control

recommended_actions: Disable `enable_exception_autocapture` for the telemetry client, or route exceptions through a sanitizer that strips paths, filenames, and snippets before send.
Document precisely what the opted-in telemetry transmits so the remit's "no code/PII" guarantee is verifiable.

positives.title: No autonomous git-push capability
positives.description: No push method exists anywhere in the scanned package — the remit's forbidden autonomous push is prevented by the capability simply being absent, not merely gated.
positives.evidence_path: aider/repo.py (no push method; grep of commands.py/base_coder.py)

positives.title: LLM-proposed shell commands require explicit, non-blanket confirmation
positives.description: Suggested shell commands are confirmed with explicit_yes_required=True and the command shown verbatim as the subject; even --yes-always auto-declines rather than auto-approves, and a blanket "all" answer is disabled for these prompts.

positives.title: Dependencies fully pinned via compiled lockfile
positives.description: requirements.txt is generated by uv pip compile with == pins against a committed common-constraints.txt, closing dependency-confusion and version-swap exposure.

positives.title: Secrets and transcripts force-excluded from git and file-watch
positives.description: .env and .aider* are always ignored by the file watcher and .env is auto-added to .gitignore, keeping credentials and conversation transcripts out of version control and out of watch-triggered context.

positives.title: Bounded auto-fix reflection loop
positives.description: Automatic lint/test-fix reflection is capped at max_reflections=3 and enforced, preventing runaway self-retry loops.

positives.title: Opt-in analytics with model-name redaction
positives.description: Telemetry is opt-in / percentage-sampled and unknown model names are redacted before events are sent, limiting routine metadata exposure.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. Aider has a coherent, operative safety model — a real confirmation gate on LLM-proposed shell execution, no autonomous push, fully pinned dependencies, and `.env`/`.aider*` force-excluded from git and file-watch — that lifts it above hobby-project territory. Its weaknesses are concentrated in Zero Trust: the confirmation model is bypassable for package installs and out-of-repo writes under `--yes-always`, path confinement is prompt-mediated rather than code-enforced, and untrusted repository content reaches the model with no neutralization. Supply-chain hygiene is the strongest category; adversarial-testing evidence is the weakest.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Aider is structurally scoped to a single interactive session against one git repository and its tool inventory matches the remit's Known Good Baseline, but it is a general-purpose code assistant with no topic gate and its repository-root confinement (`abs_root_path`, `cmd_add`) is prompt-mediated and bypassable rather than a hard boundary.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: `.env` and `.aider*` transcripts are force-excluded from git and the file-watcher, but untrusted content — file bodies, `AI!`/`AI?` comments, git history, commit messages, and scraped web pages — enters the LLM context through `watch.py` and the scrape/read paths with no validation, labeling, or secret redaction.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: A real code-level gate confirms LLM-proposed shell commands with the command shown verbatim and cannot be blanket-approved by `--yes-always`, but the same protection is missing on package installs and file create/edit prompts (`default="y"`, no `explicit_yes_required`), auto-commit runs with no pre-commit review, and out-of-repo writes are not code-confined.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: `requirements.txt` is fully `==`-pinned via `uv pip compile` against a committed constraints file, inference is mediated through the maintained `litellm` dependency, and no live credentials live in workspace files (the PostHog/Mixpanel keys in `analytics.py` are public client ingestion keys).

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No adversarial-testing artifacts, injection-test fixtures, or red-team evidence appear in the scanned scope (`aider/*.py` and `aider/coders/`), and nothing indicates that prompt-injection or confirmation-bypass scenarios drove the current design; a broader test suite exists in the repo but outside this scan's scope.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Aider writes durable local transcripts (`.aider.chat.history.md`, `.aider.llm.history`, `.aider.input.history`) and opt-in structured analytics events, but these are free-form transcripts capturing full content rather than a structured, action-level security log, and there is no alerting on high-impact actions.
